title: Marble cake
subtitle: Chocolate and vanilla batters swirled together in a loaf
excerpt: Two separate batters, one plain vanilla and one with cocoa, spooned into the same tin and swirled with a skewer before baking. The marbled cut reveals the pattern — a classic British and European cake with a dense, buttery crumb.
sourceNotes: Marble cake (Marmorkuchen in German) is documented in German and Austro-Hungarian domestic cookery from the mid-nineteenth century, where it is one of the standard Kuchens alongside Gugelhupf. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136, includes marble cake as a domestic showpiece made by layering differently coloured batters. The technique of swirling (rather than layering) using a skewer is the modern domestic adaptation.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze sliced. Thaw at room temperature.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps wrapped for 4 days at room temperature. Improves slightly on the second day.

content.text: Marble cake starts from a single creamed batter, split and flavoured differently. A quarter of the batter has cocoa stirred through; the larger plain portion stays vanilla. The two batters are spooned into the tin in alternating dollops, then a skewer or knife is drawn through the tin once or twice in a figure-of-eight to create the marble without fully mixing the two. Too many strokes and the batters merge; too few and the marble is coarse and irregular.

Pull a skewer through the batter in two to three long, slow passes in a figure-of-eight motion. The aim is to create ribbons, not to blend. A skewer pulled too quickly or too many times will produce a uniform brown-grey batter rather than a distinct marble.

Heat the oven to 180°C (160°C fan). Line a 900 g loaf tin with baking paper. Beat the {{unsalted-butter}} and {{caster-sugar}} together until pale and fluffy, about 4 minutes. Beat in the {{whole-eggs}} one at a time. Fold in the {{self-raising-flour}} and {{whole-milk}} (first measure) until just combined. Stir in the {{vanilla-extract}}.

Spoon about one-quarter of the batter into a separate bowl. Stir in the {{cocoa-powder}} and the extra {{whole-milk}} (second measure) until smooth and combined. Spoon alternate dollops of the vanilla and chocolate batters into the prepared tin, varying the order to create a random pattern. Draw a skewer or butter knife through the batter in two slow figure-of-eight passes to create the marble.

Bake for 50-55 minutes until a skewer inserted in the centre comes out clean, the top is deep golden, and the cake has pulled away from the sides of the tin. If the top is colouring too fast, cover loosely with foil after 35 minutes. Cool in the tin for 15 minutes, then turn out onto a rack and cool completely before slicing.
